does: This paper proposes Diffusion LAIR, a listwise preference optimization method that moves beyond pairwise comparisons for aligning text-to-image diffusion models. Instead of reducing supervision to binary winner-loser labels, the method takes a group of candidate images for each prompt and converts their reward scores into centered advantage weights. It then optimizes an advantage-weighted regression objective on the implicit reward, defined as the denoising-loss improvement of the current model relative to a fixed reference model, with a quadratic penalty to regularize reward magnitude. The approach leverages all candidate images simultaneously rather than selecting pairs, enabling the model to learn from richer reward information while maintaining conservative behavior through explicit regularization.
connects: This work builds on the recent shift from online RLHF to offline preference optimization for diffusion models, responding to limitations of purely pairwise comparison methods. It extends prior research in preference-based learning by incorporating listwise ranking objectives similar to those used in information retrieval and ranking problems. The method combines insights from reward modeling in RL with the implicit reward framework developed for diffusion model alignment, while addressing the practical observation that training datasets often naturally contain multiple candidates per prompt rather than isolated pairs. The approach also relates to advantage-weighted regression techniques from policy optimization, adapted here to the diffusion setting.
enables: This work opens avenues for more efficient alignment of diffusion models by better utilizing multi-candidate training data that typically exists in preference datasets. It suggests directions for extending listwise methods to other structured reward scenarios, such as ranked lists or ratings with varying confidence levels. The explicit regularization of implicit rewards could inform future work on stability in preference optimization and understanding the relationship between reward learning and model alignment. The method potentially enables improved sample efficiency in alignment by extracting more information from existing preference data, and could inspire similar listwise approaches in other generative model families or domains where natural grouping of candidates occurs.
